title: Understanding Animal Navigation Through Magnetic Fields

lesson.learn: Over 20 species of animals, including migratory birds, sharks, and monarch butterflies, possess an extraordinary ability to perceive and navigate through magnetic fields. This unique sensory power allows them to orient themselves, migrate, and even find food. Research has revealed that animals detect the Earth's magnetic field using specialized cells called magnetite, which is capable of detecting changes in the magnetic field. Magnetite occurs naturally in some biological tissues, including the brain, heart, and organs of many animals.
lesson.simpler: Some animals use a built-in magnetoreceptive system to navigate, rather than relying on visual or chemical cues. This system is thought to be mediated by magnetite, a biomineral that is capable of detecting the Earth's magnetic field. In this way, animals can 'see' magnetic fields without using their eyes.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The discovery of magnetoreception in animals has provided new insights into the evolution of navigation and sensory systems in these species. By understanding how animals use magnetic fields to navigate, scientists can better comprehend the complex interactions between animals, their environment, and the Earth's magnetic field.
lesson.deeperDive: Studies have shown that migratory birds, such as the European robin and the Arctic tern, use the Earth's magnetic field to guide their migrations across continents. Some species of sharks, including the spiny dogfish and the great white shark, can detect the magnetic field and use it to navigate through the ocean. Even some insects, like monarch butterflies, possess magnetoreceptive abilities. This unique sensory power is thought to be mediated by magnetite, which is capable of detecting changes in the magnetic field.
lesson.funFact: Researchers have discovered that some animals can detect the strength and direction of magnetic fields, as well as changes in the magnetic field over time.

places: The Natural History Museum in London
The University of California, San Diego
years: 1960: The discovery of magnetite in biological tissues marked the beginning of the study of animal magnetoreception
1994: Researchers first demonstrated that migratory birds can detect and use the Earth's magnetic field for navigation
2018: A study published in the journal Science revealed that even some insects possess magnetoreceptive abilities
concepts: Magnetite: a biomineral that is thought to mediate animal magnetoreception
Magnetic fields: invisible forces that permeate the Earth and other celestial bodies
Compass effect: the ability of animals to detect changes in the magnetic field and use it as a navigational tool
Animal navigation: the ability of animals to orient themselves and move through their environment using sensory information
Magnetoreception: the ability of animals to detect magnetic fields using specialized cells called magnetite
